title: Definition, measurement, and functions of money
description: Practice and gain better understanding of the money supply and the purpose of money.
topic_path: Definition, measurement, and functions of money
Definition, measurement, and functions of money

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best defines money?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Any asset that is accepted as a way of paying for something
Paper currency or coins that are backed in value by gold
Currency that has intrinsic value, such as gold coins

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains why a \$20 bill is money but a share of stock is not money?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The \$20 bill can be used as a medium of exchange, but the share of stock cannot be used to purchase anything.
The \$20 bill is a store of value, but the share of stock is risky.
The \$20 bill does not earn income, but the share of stock does earn income.
The \$20 bill is an illiquid asset, but the share of stock is a liquid asset.
The \$20 bill is not an asset, but a share of stock is an asset.

assessment_items.question_content:
Eliza buried money in the backyard to use in an emergency.

**Which of the three functions of money is described here?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which of the following is a function of money that is most immediately impacted by hyperinflation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which of the following best describes the difference between fiat money and commodity money?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: commodity money has intrinsic value, but fiat money does not
commodity money can act as a medium of exchange, but fiat money cannot
commodity money can act as a unit of account, but fiat money cannot
commodity money can serve all three functions of money, but fiat money is only a medium of exchange
commodity money cannot serve as a medium of exchange, but fiat money can

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the monetary aggregate M1?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Currency in circulation + Demand deposits + travelers checks
Currency in circulation + Demand deposits + travelers checks + savings accounts
Currency in circulation + Demand deposits + travelers checks + Currency in vaults
Currency in circulation + gold bricks + currency in vaults
Currency in circulation + Demand deposits + travelers checks + small time deposits

assessment_items.question_content:
The values of several types of assets in Hamsterville are given in the table below.

Asset	| Value (in millions)
-|-
Cash in vaults at banks	|$\$100$
Cash held by the public	|$\$200$
Traveler’s checks		|$\$20$
Balance of all checkable deposits |$\$400$
Balance of all savings accounts |$\$300$
Certificates of deposits (CDs) |$\$10$
Money market mutual funds |$\$5$


**What is the value of M2 in Hamsterville?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
M2 is the broadest definition of the money supply.
M2 contains all of M1—currency in circulation, traveler’s checks, savings accounts, and checkable bank deposits—and also less liquid assets such as certificates of deposit, and money market mutual fund balances.
Cash held in vaults is not in circulation and, therefore, is not counted in M2.
The value of each of the components of M2 is listed below in millions:

Asset	| Value (in millions)
-|-
Currency in circulation |$\$200$
Traveler’s checks |$\$20$
Checkable bank deposits |$\$400$
Balance of all savings accounts 	|$\$300$
CDs |$\$10$
Money market mutual fund balances |$\$5$
$\begin{align}M2&=\$200 + \$20 + \$400 + \$300 +\$10 + \$5\\\\
&=\$935\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The values of several types of assets in Hamsterville are given in the table below.

Asset|Value (in millions)
-|-
Cash in vaults at banks		|$\$100$
Cash held by the public		|$\$200$
Traveler’s checks			|$\$20$
Balance of all checkable deposits |$\$400$
Balance of all savings accounts 	|$\$300$
Certificates of deposits (CDs) 	|$\$10$
Money market mutual funds 	|$\$5$

**What is the value of M1 in Hamsterville?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
M1 is the narrowest definition of the money supply.
M1 contains currency in circulation, traveler’s checks, savings accounts, and checkable bank deposits.
Cash held in vaults is not in circulation.
The value of each of the components of M1 is listed below in millions:

Asset| Value
-|-
Currency in circulation |$\$200$
Traveler’s checks |$\$20$
Checkable bank deposits |$\$400$
Balance of all savings accounts 	|$\$300$
$\begin{align}M1&= \$200 + \$20 + \$400 + \$300\\\\
&=\$920 \text{ million}\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is included in the monetary base?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Money held in bank vaults and money held by the public
All deposits in banks and money in circulation
Any asset of any value including cash, stocks, and bonds
Only money held by the public

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are included in the monetary base (MB), but NOT  in the money supply (M1)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Currency in bank vaults on reserve

assessment_items.question_content:
Felipe deposits $\$100$ in the bank. The bank stores $\$20$ of his deposit in a vault and will loan out other $\$80$.

**The $\$20$ stored in the bank vault is part of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The values of several types of assets in Hamsterville are given in the table below.


Asset		|Value (in millions)
-|-
Cash in vaults at banks	|$\$100$
Cash held by the public	|$\$200$
Traveler’s checks		|$\$20$
Balance of all checkable deposits |$\$400$
Balance of all savings accounts |$\$300$
Certificates of deposits (CDs) |$\$10$
Money market mutual funds |$\$5$

**What is the value of the monetary base in Hamsterville?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The monetary base includes money in circulation and money that can potentially be put into circulation.
Money held in reserve at banks can potentially be put into circulation.
The monetary base includes cash in circulation and money held in bank vaults.
The monetary base is $\$300$:

$\begin{align}\text{Monetary Base}&=\text{Cash in circulation} +\text{Money held in vaults}\\\\
&=\$100 + \$200\\\\
&=\$300\end{align}$
